<!-- Topic 3: Dangling Pointers After delete -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# Dangling Pointers After delete

---

## What's left behind when you delete? {.smaller}

+ `delete` frees the memory block — but the pointer variable still holds the old address.
+ Using that address afterward is undefined behavior.

::: notes
Total slides: 4
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The dangling pointer problem

```{.cpp}
int* p = new int(42);
delete p;

cout << *p << "\n";   // undefined behavior — memory is freed
*p = 99;              // undefined behavior — writing to freed memory
```

- The memory at that address may have been reused by another allocation
- The program may crash, produce wrong output, or appear to work — unpredictably
- The compiler will not warn you; this is a runtime problem

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## The fix: nullptr immediately after delete

```{.cpp}
int* p = new int(42);

// ... use p ...

delete p;
p = nullptr;          // safe — dereferencing nullptr crashes loudly

if (p != nullptr) {
    cout << *p;       // guarded access
}
```

- A null pointer dereference produces a predictable crash — easier to debug than silent corruption
- Make `delete` + `nullptr` a single habit, not two separate thoughts
- Does not help with alias pointers (another variable pointing to the same block)

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Summary

+ A dangling pointer holds the address of freed memory — using it is undefined behavior.
+ Set every pointer to `nullptr` immediately after `delete`; a null dereference crashes loudly and predictably, which is better than silent corruption.

<!-- Slide 4 -->
